title: Materials and Discontinuities: Employer-Defined Applications
narrationScript:
Walk up to any part on the bench and it carries a history. It was melted, cast, maybe rolled or forged, maybe welded, machined, heat treated, and then it went to work. A discontinuity can enter at any point in that story. Your job gets much easier when you know which chapter you are reading, because the origin of a discontinuity predicts where it sits and what it looks like.

We sort discontinuities into three families. Inherent discontinuities form when the metal first solidifies, in the ingot or the casting. Processing discontinuities form during manufacturing, when the material is shaped, welded, machined, or heat treated. Service-induced discontinuities form after the part goes to work, under load, heat, and a corrosive world.

Start with inherent. Molten metal holds dissolved gas, and as it freezes that gas comes out as porosity, rounded voids in the metal. The melt also carries nonmetallic material, like oxides and slag. Those freeze in place as inclusions. Metal shrinks as it solidifies, and if fresh liquid cannot feed a thick section, you get shrinkage cavities. If two streams of liquid metal meet but are too cold to fuse, you get a cold shut, which looks like a smooth crease in a casting surface. Alloying elements can also distribute unevenly, which we call segregation. Now here is the part people miss. When an ingot full of those flaws gets rolled into plate or bar, the flaws do not vanish. They flatten and stretch. Porosity and inclusions become laminations inside plate, seams along bar, and stringers of inclusions. They are still inherent. They are just wearing a new shape.

Processing comes next. Forging and rolling can fold surface metal over and press it in without fusion. That is a lap. Heavy working can rupture the inside of a bar. That is a burst. Welding deserves its own paragraph, because welds are where visual testing earns its keep. At the surface of a finished weld you can see undercut, a groove melted into the base metal at the toe. You can see overlap, weld metal that rolled over the base metal without fusing. You can see surface porosity, spatter, arc strikes, and reinforcement that stands too tall. And you can see cracks, in the crater at the end of a bead, along the weld, across it, or in the heat affected zone. After welding, secondary operations add their own list. Abusive grinding heat checks a hardened surface with fine cracks. Heat treating and quenching can crack a part at sharp corners. Plating can charge the surface with hydrogen and crack it later.

Then the part goes to work, and service takes over. Cyclic loads grow fatigue cracks, and they almost always start at a stress riser, a hole, a notch, a weld toe, a sharp fillet. Corrosion shows up as general thinning, as pitting, as crevice attack under gaskets and deposits, as galvanic attack where two different metals touch, and as stress corrosion cracking where tension and the wrong chemistry meet. Flowing fluid erodes elbows. Collapsing vapor bubbles hammer pump impellers, and we call that cavitation. Long heat and stress stretch metal slowly, which is creep. Add wear and plain mechanical damage and you have the service family.

Now, materials. Most of this course talks steel, but the same eyes work on other materials, and your employer defines which ones apply to your job. Mineral-based materials include concrete, ceramics, glass, and refractory brick. On concrete you look for cracking, spalling, scaling, and rust staining that betrays corroding rebar underneath. On ceramics and glass you look for chips, checks, and bubbles. Organic-based materials include plastics, composites, and rubber. Plastics craze, which is a web of fine surface cracks. Molded parts show sink marks, voids, and knit lines. Composites delaminate at edges and drilled holes, and show resin starved, fuzzy areas. Rubber cracks with age and ozone. The written practice and your procedure tell you which materials, which products, and which discontinuities you are responsible for. That is what employer-defined application means.

Here is the habit to build. Before you look at the part, ask what has happened to it. Cast or wrought. Welded or not. New, or ten years in service. The answer tells you what to hunt and where to hunt it. That is the difference between looking and inspecting.

sections.heading: Three Families, One Question: When Did It Form?
sections.body:
Walk up to any part and it carries a history: melted and solidified, then rolled, forged, machined, welded, or heat treated, and finally put to work. A discontinuity — any interruption in the normal structure of the material — can enter at every stage. We group them into three families by origin:

- **Inherent** — formed during the original melting and solidification of the ingot or casting.
- **Processing** — formed during manufacturing operations: rolling, forging, welding, machining, grinding, heat treating, plating.
- **Service-induced** — formed after the part entered service, under load, temperature, chemistry, flow, or abuse.

Why the taxonomy matters: origin predicts both **where** a discontinuity sits and **what it looks like**. A fatigue crack lives at a stress riser. A lamination shows on a cut plate edge. Undercut lives at a weld toe. When your employer defines the applications you inspect — specific products, materials, and processes — the procedure is really telling you which families to hunt and where to hunt them. One vocabulary note before we start: a discontinuity is only a **defect** after it has been evaluated against acceptance criteria and found rejectable. We close that loop in the last lesson of this module.

sections.caption: Each stage of a material's life leaves its own characteristic discontinuities, and earlier flaws can ride along unseen until a later operation exposes them.

sections.heading: Inherent: Born in the Melt
sections.body:
Molten metal is a messy place. As it freezes, dissolved gases come out of solution and leave **porosity** — rounded voids, single or clustered. Nonmetallic material in the melt — oxides, sulfides, slag — freezes in place as **inclusions**. Metal shrinks as it solidifies; when a heavy section cannot be fed with fresh liquid, it pulls itself apart internally and leaves **shrinkage** cavities, often spongy and branched. Two streams of metal that meet too cold to fuse leave a **cold shut**: a smooth, crease-like seam on a casting surface. Uneven distribution of alloying elements is **segregation**, which can show as banding after machining or etching and locally changes properties.

Wrought products inherit these flaws. When the ingot is rolled or drawn, internal porosity and inclusions flatten and stretch:

- **Laminations** — thin, flat separations parallel to a plate surface; hunt for them on cut and sheared edges.
- **Seams** — tight, longitudinal surface discontinuities in bar and tubing, often opening up after machining.
- **Stringers** — elongated lines of inclusions running with the working direction.

They show up in the mill product, but their origin is the melt — so they are still classed as inherent.

sections.heading: Processing: Shaped, Welded, Ground, and Quenched
sections.body:
Primary processing adds its own list. A **lap** is surface metal folded over during forging or rolling and pressed flat without fusion — it reads as a tight, shallow crack lying at a low angle to the surface. A **burst** is an internal or surface rupture from working a section too hard or at the wrong temperature.

Welds are where Level I visual work concentrates, because so much is visible right at the surface:

- **Undercut** — a groove melted into the base metal along the toe and left unfilled.
- **Overlap** — weld metal that rolled past the toe onto the base metal without fusing.
- **Excess reinforcement** — a cap stacked higher than the limit; height is measured, not guessed.
- **Surface porosity** — pores and wormholes breaking the cap or root surface.
- **Cracks** — in the crater at a bead's end, longitudinal along the weld, transverse across it, or in the heat-affected zone.
- **Spatter and arc strikes** — expelled droplets stuck to base metal, and stray arcing damage beside the joint; both can crack hardenable steels.
- **Incomplete penetration or fusion** — visible only where the root or joint face is accessible.

Secondary processing closes the list: abusive **grinding** heat-checks hardened surfaces with networks of fine cracks, **heat-treat and quench cracks** open at sharp corners and section changes, and **plating** can charge a surface with hydrogen that cracks it after the fact.

sections.caption: Surface weld discontinuities in plan view and cross-section — the core recognition set for Level I weld examination.

sections.heading: Service-Induced: Earned in the Field
sections.body: Once hardware enters service, the environment goes to work. **Fatigue cracks** grow under cyclic load and start at stress risers — bolt holes, weld toes, sharp fillets, tool marks. At the surface they read as tight, clean hairlines with no deformation around them. **Corrosion** wears many faces: **uniform** thinning over broad areas; **pitting** — small-mouthed, deep cavities far worse than they look; **crevice** attack under gaskets, clamps, and deposits where stagnant chemistry concentrates; **galvanic** attack where dissimilar metals couple; **intergranular** attack along grain boundaries; and **stress-corrosion cracking** — branched cracking that needs sustained tension plus a specific environment, with chlorides on austenitic stainless the classic pairing. **Erosion** thins elbows and impellers where flow turns or accelerates; **cavitation** chews pump internals with collapsing vapor bubbles; **creep** slowly stretches and swells metal in long, hot, stressed service; **hydrogen damage** embrittles and blisters; **wear and fretting** polish, groove, and pit mating surfaces; and **mechanical damage** — dents, gouges, overload distortion — needs no introduction. In-service VT is usually a hunt for change: compare against the baseline record, go to the stress risers first, and look under everything that traps moisture.

sections.caption: Quick reference: the three origin families and where to hunt them.
rows: Porosity, inclusions, shrinkage, cold shuts, segregation; laminations, seams, stringers in wrought product
Casting surfaces, machined faces, plate and sheared edges, bar after machining
Laps, bursts; weld undercut, overlap, cracks, porosity, spatter, arc strikes; grinding, quench, and plating cracks
Forged surfaces, welds and the heat-affected zone, ground or hardened surfaces
Fatigue cracks; uniform, pitting, crevice, galvanic, and stress-corrosion attack; erosion, cavitation, creep, wear, mechanical damage
Stress risers, under deposits and gaskets, flow direction changes, wear interfaces

sections.heading: Beyond Steel: Mineral, Organic, and Employer-Defined Materials
sections.body:
The same disciplined eye transfers to other material families — and your employer's written practice defines which ones apply to you.

**Mineral-based materials.** On concrete, look for cracking patterns, **spalling** — fragments breaking away, often over corroding rebar that betrays itself first with rust staining — surface **scaling**, **honeycombing** from poor consolidation, and white **efflorescence** that marks water migration. On ceramics and refractory linings: chips, cracks, glaze crazing, and spalled or eroded brick. In glass: bubbles called seeds, solid inclusions called stones, and surface checks.

**Metallic materials**, including welds, carry the inherent and processing lists you just learned plus everything service adds — they are the core of most VT programs.

**Organic-based materials.** Plastics show **crazing** — networks of fine surface cracks from stress plus solvents or UV — along with sink marks, voids, knit lines, flash, and chalky discoloration. Composites and fiberglass show **delamination** at edges and drilled holes, fiber breakout, resin-rich or resin-starved areas, and blisters. Rubber cracks with age, ozone, and flexing.

**Other materials** are exactly what the phrase says: whatever your employer's products require — brazed and soldered joints, coatings checked for holidays, sags, and blisters, glass-lined vessels, timber checked for splits and rot. The procedure names the material, the product, and the discontinuities of record; you bring the method.

sections.title: Read the History Before You Read the Part
sections.body: Four questions before the first look: Was it **cast or wrought**? Was it **welded**? What was **done to the surface** — ground, hardened, plated? Has it **been in service**, and for how long? The answers tell you which discontinuity family to expect, where it hides, and what it looks like — so your lighting, viewing angle, and scan pattern stop being generic and start being aimed.

figures.title: Where Discontinuities Originate: The Material Life Cycle
Surface Weld Discontinuities in Plan View and Cross-Section

interactive.title: Origin Call: Inherent, Processing, or Service-Induced?
interactive.intro: Classify each discontinuity by where it entered the material's life. Think about what was happening to the material at the moment the flaw formed.

items.label: Shrinkage cavity exposed on a machined casting face
items.why: Shrinkage forms as metal contracts during solidification — it was in the material from the original melt.

items.label: Lamination on the flame-cut edge of a rolled plate
items.why: Laminations are ingot porosity and inclusions flattened by rolling. The origin is the original solidification, carried forward into the plate.

items.label: Lap on a forged crane hook
items.why: A lap is surface metal folded over and pressed in without fusion during forging or rolling — a primary processing discontinuity.

items.label: Undercut along the toe of a fillet weld
items.why: Undercut is a groove the welding arc melts into the base metal — welding is a processing operation.

items.label: Crater crack at the end of a weld bead
items.why: Crater cracks form in the shrinking puddle when the arc is broken — created during welding, so processing.

items.label: Network of fine grinding cracks on a hardened shaft
items.why: Localized grinding heat checks a hardened surface — a secondary processing discontinuity.

items.label: Fatigue crack growing from a bolt-hole edge
items.why: Fatigue requires cyclic service loads, and it starts at stress risers like hole edges — strictly service-induced.

items.label: Pitting on a tank floor under sediment deposits
items.why: Pitting is localized corrosion that develops in the service environment, classically under deposits where chemistry stagnates.

items.label: Cold shut on an as-cast pump housing
items.why: A cold shut forms when two streams of molten metal meet without fusing — an original solidification flaw.

items.label: Erosion thinning at a pipe elbow downstream of a control valve
items.why: Flow-driven metal loss accumulates in service where velocity and turbulence are highest — a service-induced condition.

checkQuestions.question: A thin, flat separation shows on the sheared edge of a steel plate, running parallel to the plate surface. Where did this discontinuity originate?
options: In the shearing operation that cut the edge
As porosity and inclusions in the original ingot, flattened during rolling
checkQuestions.explanation: That is a lamination: ingot porosity and nonmetallic inclusions squeezed flat and elongated as the ingot was rolled into plate. It is classed as inherent because its origin is the original solidification.

checkQuestions.question: Which of the following is a service-induced discontinuity?
options: Cold shut on a casting surface
Cavitation damage on a pump impeller
checkQuestions.explanation: Cavitation damage comes from vapor bubbles collapsing against the metal while the pump operates — service. The other three formed during original solidification or primary processing.

checkQuestions.question: Along the toe of a finished fillet weld you find a continuous groove melted into the base metal and left unfilled. What is it?
checkQuestions.explanation: A groove melted into the base metal at the toe and left unfilled is undercut. Overlap is the opposite geometry — weld metal lying over the base metal without fusion.

checkQuestions.question: A polycarbonate machine guard shows a fine network of shallow surface cracks after years of solvent cleaning. The correct name for this condition is:
checkQuestions.explanation: Crazing is the web of fine surface cracks polymers develop under stress combined with solvents or UV. Delamination is layer separation in composites; efflorescence and spalling belong to concrete and masonry.

checkQuestions.question: You can examine only the cap side of a completed groove weld. Which discontinuity is LEAST likely to be detected?
checkQuestions.explanation: Incomplete penetration lives at the root; with no access to the root side it is hidden from direct visual testing. The other three present right at the cap surface.

references: SNT-TC-1A — employer written practice defines applications and materials
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations (generic reference)
